segments.s559-g57: کنون is an adverb meaning 'now' placed at the sentence start for temporal framing. کار is the topic/noun 'affair/deed' and is followed by the name phrase بهرام بهرامیان; Ferdowsi omits the expected ezāfe (کارِ بهرامِ بهرامیان) in poetic diction, so بهرام بهرامیان functions as a genitive/appositive identifying whose affair it is ('Bahram, of the Bahramids').
segments.s559-g58: بگویم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/optative (literary) used to mean 'I will tell' or 'let me tell'. The imperative/imperative-like verb بشنو ('listen') addresses تو ('you'), which is placed before the verb for emphasis. به جان و روان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'with (your) heart and soul' (به + two nouns joined by و) giving the manner/intensity of listening.
segments.s559-g41: چو is the conditional/temporal particle 'when/if'. خشنود داری is a present-tense clause 'you keep (it) pleased' where داری (have/keep) is the verb and جهان را (world + object marker را) is its definite object; به داد is a prepositional phrase 'by/with justice' qualifying how you keep the world pleased. Word order is slightly poetic (verb close to the adjective 'خشنود'), but the object marker را makes the object explicit.
segments.s559-g42: توانگر بمانی is a result clause: بمانی (remain/last) in the present/subjunctive sense yields 'you will remain (as) wealthy'. و connects a coordinating clause; از داد شاد = 'happy/joyful because of justice', where از indicates cause ('because of') and شاد is the predicate adjective.
segments.s559-g43: همه is the general subject 'everyone/all'. ایمنی باید literally 'security is needed' uses باید as an impersonal modal 'it is necessary' (so: 'everyone must have security'); و راستی appends a second required quality 'and uprightness'. The copula is omitted—classical Persian often expresses necessity this way.
segments.s559-g44: نباید is the impersonal negative 'there must not / ought not'. به داد اندرون is a locative/relational phrase 'in matters of justice, within (the inner place)'; کاستی is the noun 'deficiency/lack' that follows the negation. The sequence therefore means 'there should be no deficiency of justice inside.'
segments.s559-g45: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause 'when/if'. شادی is the subject 'joy'; بکاهی is an adverbial phrase 'a little / slightly' (classical compound), and بکاهد is the present verb 'it lessens' (decreases). روان is the affected noun 'the soul/spirit'—poetic order places the verb near the adverb and the affected noun after it.
segments.s559-g46: خرد گردد is a two-word predicate: خرد 'wisdom' with گردد (becomes) giving 'wisdom becomes/comes to be'. اندر میان ناتوان is a locative phrase 'among the weak/powerless' that specifies where wisdom is reduced. گردَدن here is the intransitive copular/transformative 'become' common in classical Persian.
segments.s559-g47: چو شد introduces a temporal clause 'when it became'. پادشاهیش is 'his kingship/reign' (pādshāhī + possessive -ش). بر سال بیست is a duration expression 'to/at the year twenty'—classical Persian uses بر + سال + numeral to state duration (i.e. 'when his reign reached twenty years').
segments.s559-g48: This short, somewhat elliptical phrase (یکی کم برو زندگانی) indicates a small lack/shortness in life ('one short of [a year] in his life' / 'a little short in life') and functions adverbially; گریست is the main verb 'he wept'. Such compact expressions are common in epic diction where measure and rhythm produce elliptical phrase order.
segments.s559-g9: به یک هفته is a prepositional phrase meaning 'for one week' (duration) placed before the verb. با او 'with him' marks the companion, and بکوشید is the past (epic) 3rd‑person singular 'he strove/tried'; سخت is an adverb modifying بکوشید ('strove hard' / 'strongly').
segments.s559-g10: همی بود is a compound imperfective/past idiom ('kept being' / 'continued') where همی gives a continuous or iterative flavour and بود is the past of 'to be'. تا introduces the endpoint 'until' and heads the clause 'تا ... نشست', where نشست is the past verb 'sat' and او is its subject; بر ... به تخت ('upon/on the throne') gives the location, with بر 'upon' and به here marking motion/onto the throne.
segments.s559-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بنشست is the past verb 'sat down' (verb-before-subject is common in poetry), بهرام is the subject, بر تخت is the prepositional phrase 'on the throne'. داد here is a past verb 'gave/dispensed' used idiomatically to mean 'dispensed justice' (the object 'justice' is implicit or understood).
segments.s559-g12: برسم کیان is a frozen prepositional phrase meaning 'according to the custom/ceremony of the Kians' (بر + رسم), functioning as an adverbial. تاج is the direct object 'crown', بر سر is the locative 'on (his) head', and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'placed/put', so the verb places the crown on his head. Word order (object before verb) is typical in poetic diction.
segments.s559-g13: نخست is an adverb 'first / at first'. آفرین کرد is a light-verb construction: آفرین (praise/acclamation) + کرد (made/did) = 'praised' or 'gave praise to'. بر before کردگار functions here as a preposition meaning 'to/toward' introducing the indirect object کردگار ('the Creator').
segments.s559-g14: فروزنده is an active participle/adjectival epithet 'illuminating / making bright' modifying the following noun phrase. گردش روزگار is a genitive phrase 'the turning/course of time' (گردش = turning/circuit; روزگار = time/fate), so together this is an epithet of the Creator: 'the illuminator of the course of time.'
segments.s559-g15: فزاینده is an active participle 'increasing/augmenting' modifying the coordinated nouns دانش و راستی ('knowledge and uprightness'). The construction is a terse epithet: 'increaser of knowledge and of rightness.'
segments.s559-g16: گزاینده is an active participle meaning 'one who removes/banishes' (here used as an epithet); it takes کژی و کاستی ('crookedness and deficiency') as its objects. The line uses parallel structure: first 'furthering' virtues, then 'removing' vices and lacks. (Note: the provided lemma senses for گزاینده did not include this 'remover' sense.)
segments.s559-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning 'when' introducing a time clause. The sequence بهرام در سوک بهرامشاه reads 'Bahram in the mourning (for) Bahramshah' — در سوک = 'in mourning for' and the name بهرامشاه is the object of that mourning.
segments.s559-g2: چهل روز is an adverbial duration 'for forty days'. ننهاد is a past negated verb (3rd person) meaning 'did not place/put on'. بر سر کلاه is the usual Persian collocation 'کلاه بر سر نهادن' (to put a hat on the head) but inverted here; word order is poetic—object and location follow the verb.
segments.s559-g3: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they departed/went away'. گردان بسیار is a noun phrase 'many troops / a great company' (بسیار quantifies گردان). هوش is used as a noun describing their mental state ('mind/awareness') attached to that group.
segments.s559-g4: پر از درد is a prepositional phrase 'full of pain/sorrow' (پر = 'full (of)', از = 'of', درد = 'sorrow/pain'). با ناله و با خروش are parallel instrumental/associative phrases 'with moaning and with cries' modifying how they left.
segments.s559-g5: نشستند is the past plural 'they sat' (here meaning 'they sat down / took their place' in mourning). با او indicates accompaniment 'with him'. به سوک و به درد are prepositional phrases 'in mourning and in sorrow' describing the manner of their sitting.
segments.s559-g6: دو رخ زرد = 'their two faces / both cheeks were yellow/pale' (دو رخ literally 'two faces'); رخ is 'face', زرد here means 'pale/wan' in a mournful context. لبها شده لاژورد = 'the lips became lapis-blue' — شده is a perfect/resultative 'have become' and لاژورد names the blue lapis color.
segments.s559-g7: وزان پس is a temporal/contiguous phrase 'and/thereafter' (وزان = 'from that/thereupon', پس = 'then/after'). بشد is a past/formative verb 'there became / arose'. موبد پاک‌رای is the predicate noun 'a mobed (Zoroastrian priest) of pure reputation' — adjective follows the noun.
segments.s559-g8: که introduces a relative clause modifying موبد ('who/that ...'). گیرد is a present/subjunctive sense of 'take/seize' (here 'to place, set'). مگر is used classically as a particle in the clause (here read as concessive/exceptional; in context it helps mark the action targeting the king). شاه بر گاه جای literally 'the king onto the throne-place' (بر + گاه + جای = 'upon the throne'). The whole clause explains the priest's function: to place the king upon the throne.
segments.s559-g33: مروت نیابد = verb phrase with مروت (the abstract noun 'muruwwat', i.e. chivalry/generosity) as grammatical subject and نیابد (negative present/optative 3rd‑person form of یابیدن) as the verb: 'is not found/attained'. کرا is an archaic relative ('for whom' / 'who') and چیز نیست = 'there is nothing' (literally 'a thing is not'), together forming 'who has nothing'. The whole segment reads literally 'muruwwat is not found for one who has nothing.'
segments.s559-g34: همان (demonstrative 'that same') modifies جاه ('rank/status'). نزد is the preposition 'near/to' and کسش is کس + possessive suffix -ش meaning 'his person' (i.e. 'with him' / 'available to him'); نیز 'also' precedes the final نیست 'is not'. Word order places the demonstrative before the noun and the prepositional phrase after it: 'that same rank is also not near his person.'
segments.s559-g35: چو here introduces a conditional 'if/when'. خشنود باشی is a subjunctive/conditional clause ('if you are content/pleased') with باشی (be) in the subordinate form. تن‌آسان is a compound adjective 'easy-bodied' = 'comfortable' and شوی (become) is the 2nd‑person verb: 'you become comfortable'.
segments.s559-g36: وگر = 'and/but if' (conditional). آز is the noun 'greed' and ورزی (from ورزیدن) is the 2nd‑person present/subjunctive form 'you practise' or 'you exert' (here 'practice greed'). هراسان is an adjective 'frightened/anxious' and شوی is again 'you become': 'but if you practise greed, you become frightened.'
segments.s559-g37: کوشیدنی is a nominal/adjectival form meaning 'that which is to be striven for / worth striving for'. کان is an archaic particle used like 'that' (introducing the thing referred to). برآرد is 3rd‑person present 'brings forth/attains' and نه at the start negates the clause: 'nor does he, by toil (به رنج), bring about/attain that which is worth striving for.' Note به رنج uses به + noun to mean 'by/with toil'.
segments.s559-g38: روان را uses را to mark روان ('the soul/spirit') as definite object. به + پیچاند with پیچاند (3rd‑person verb 'to twist/turn') expresses causing/turning ('turns the soul to/against'). از introduces source/reason 'out of' and آز گنج = 'treasure out of greed' (i.e. toward treasure because of greed). The clause describes the soul being turned toward treasure by greed.
segments.s559-g39: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' and here introduces the domain 'ز کار زمانه' = 'in/from the affairs of the age/world'. میانه گزین is an imperative/advice: میانه 'the middle/moderation' + گزین (imperative 'choose') = 'choose the middle (course/moderation)'. The imperative form is stylistically typical in advice lines.
segments.s559-g40: چو introduces a conditional 'if'. خواهی 'you want/wish' + که + یابی (subjunctive/optative 'you find/obtain') forms 'if you want that you may find/obtain ...'. بداد آفرین is a compact/poetic phrase; its precise classical reading is ambiguous here (see note). Grammatically the clause is a conditional wishing to obtain something 'by/through' the following quality; the verb forms are present/subjunctive appropriate to a protasis/apodosis pair.
segments.s559-g49: شد is the perfective/becoming verb 'became' placed before the noun phrase in archaic/poetic word order. آن تاجور شاه is a genitival/title phrase 'that merchant-king' and با خاک جفت is a compact idiom meaning 'paired with the earth' (i.e. buried), where با + noun expresses accompaniment/union.
segments.s559-g50: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; خرم جهان is the phrase 'joyful world'; دخمه (tomb) is the noun subject of the small verbless clause and بودش is the enclitic past copula meaning 'there was for him' (it existed/was his). نهفت is an adjective/past participle 'hidden/concealed' describing the tomb. Together: 'a tomb was hidden for him from the joyful world.'
segments.s559-g51: جهان را uses را as a topical/definite marker ('as for the world'); چنین است is the predicate 'is thus' (standard word order in poetry places est/’is' after the topic). آیین و ساز are coordinated nouns 'custom/rite and tune/melody' describing the world's manner.
segments.s559-g52: ندارد is the present negative 'does not have' (used impersonally here); به مرگ 'to/for death' and از کسی 'from anyone' mark the semantic roles, while چنگ باز literally 'open/claw' is an idiomatic image: death does not relinquish its claw. The whole clause means 'death does not release anyone.'
segments.s559-g53: This is an older Persian existential/possessive order: 'پسر بود او را' = 'he had a son' (بود used as past copula/existential). یکی شادکام is a following apposition 'one, fortunate' describing that son.
segments.s559-g54: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; بهرام بهرامیان داشت نام is an older way of saying 'had the name Bahram Bahramian' (داشت + نام = 'was named/held the name'). The sequence means 'whose name was Bahram (of the Bahramians).'
segments.s559-g55: بیامد نشست is a two-verb sequence 'he came and sat' (both past forms, common in narrative). از بر تخت uses the poetic locative 'از بر' meaning 'on/upon the throne,' and شاد qualifies the throne as 'joyous/happy.'
segments.s559-g56: کلاه کیانی is a titulary compound 'Kayanian cap/crown'; به سر is the goal 'to (his) head' and بر نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'placed on' (بر + نهاد = 'put upon'). The clause therefore means 'he placed the Kayanian crown on his head.'
segments.s559-g17: This noun phrase names the divine lord with two epithets: 'خداوند' (lord/sovereign) followed by two modifiers linked by 'و'. Word order is noun + modifiers, where 'کیوان' (here a proper/astral name) and 'گردان سپهر' (a genitive/attributive phrase meaning 'revolving sky/heavens') jointly describe the lord.
segments.s559-g18: Main clause: the negative present/future verb 'نخواهد' (does not/will not want) governs the object introduced by the preposition 'ز' (= 'from'). The pattern 'نخواهد ... جز ...' means 'wants nothing ... except ...'. 'به' here marks the indirect object/beneficiary, 'داد و مهر' are the two nouns joined by 'و' functioning as the only things asked for.
segments.s559-g19: 'ازان پس' is a temporal connector ('thereupon/after that'), 'چنین گفت' literally 'thus he said' — a formula introducing speech. 'کای' is the vocative 'O' addressing the audience; 'بخردان' is the vocative plural 'wise ones' (lit. 'those possessing wisdom'). Note: 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' introducing direct speech.
segments.s559-g20: A string of epithets describing the addressees: 'جهاندیده' ('world‑seeing' = worldly‑wise), coordinated with 'پاک‌دل' ('pure‑hearted'), followed by the noun 'موبدان' (mōbeds, Zoroastrian priests). Adjectives precede the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s559-g21: 'شما' (you) followed by 'هرک' ('whoever/whichever of you') forms a limiting relative; 'دارید' is the present indicative 2nd‑person plural 'you have'. 'دانش بزرگ' is the direct object 'great learning/knowledge' — adjective after noun is acceptable in poetry but here adjective follows.
segments.s559-g22: 'مباشید' is the negative jussive/imperative second‑person plural 'do not be / do not become / do not associate'. It governs the prepositional phrase 'با شهریاران سترگ' ('with / among ...'). The whole segment is a prohibitive command addressed to those who have learning.
segments.s559-g23: 'به فرهنگ یازد' is a poetic expression meaning 'in the record/chronicle there appeared' (verb 'یازد' = 'broke forth/appeared'). 'کسی' is the indefinite 'someone'; the noun cluster 'کش خرد' uses the suffix‑like element 'کش' (here agentive/possessive: 'one bearing/possessing') attached to 'خرد' (wisdom), so together 'a man possessing wisdom'.
segments.s559-g24: Predicate clause with copula 'بود' (here past 'was'). 'روشن' functions as an adjectival predicate ('clear/enlightened'), coordinated with 'مردمی' ('of the people' / 'popular, concerned with the folk') modifying 'پرورد' — here a participial/agentive sense 'nurturer' or 'one who nurtured'. The clause summarizes the man's qualities.
segments.s559-g25: سر here is figurative: 'the chief/primary (quality)'. مردمی qualifies that chief quality ('with respect to the people' or 'toward the people'), so the phrase reads 'the chief thing regarding the people was بردباری.' بود is the past copula 'was.' Note the implicit ezāfe-like relation (سرِ مردمی بردباری) common in older Persian.
segments.s559-g26: چو introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when / if'). تندی (fierceness, rashness) plus کند (the present/simple verb 'does/makes') forms a verbal phrase 'acts rashly / becomes rash.' تن به خواری بود is a small clause where تن ('person/self') is linked by the preposition به to خواری ('into humiliation'), with بود the past copula 'was.'
segments.s559-g27: هرانکس که is an indefinite relative 'whoever, he who'; گشت is the past of شدن 'became' and governs the adjective ایمن ('secure'). The second clause او شاد شد is the main clause: او (he) + شاد (happy) + شد (became) — straightforward past predication following the subordinate clause.
segments.s559-g28: غم و رنج is a coordinated subject ('sorrow and suffering'). با ایمنی is a prepositional adjunct 'with safety.' باد is the archaic optative particle ('may' / expressing a wish or a resulting state) and is followed by شد ('became' / 'were made'), so the construction conveys that sorrow and suffering were removed/dispelled by that safety (an archaic/wishful flavor possible).
segments.s559-g29: توانگرتر is the comparative form 'more wealthy.' آن کو is an archaic relative phrase meaning 'the one who.' دلی راد داشت = 'had a generous/noble heart' where دلی راد is an adjectival phrase ('big-hearted, generous') and داشت is the past verb 'had/possessed.' The clause literally: 'more wealthy is he who had a generous heart.'
segments.s559-g30: درم گرد کردن is a verbal phrase: درم (small coin) + گرد کردن, where گرد کردن here functions as 'to circulate / to scatter / to distribute' (a compound verbal idea). به دل is an adverbial/benefactive phrase 'from the heart' or 'to please the heart.' باد داشت is an older periphrasis where باد (optative) + داشت gives a desiderative/habitual sense like 'would / was wont to' or 'might be in the habit of' — here describing customary charitable action.
segments.s559-g31: اگر is the conditional 'if.' نیستت is the older contracted form 'is not to you' = 'you do not have' (verb نیست with the enclitic -ت indicating 'to you'). چیز 'thing' is the direct object, and لختی is an adverbial 'a little / for a short while' (here 'a little [something]'). بورز is the imperative of ورزیدن 'do, practice, give' — so the clause means 'If you have nothing, bestow/give a little.'
segments.s559-g32: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for / because'). بی‌چیز کس is 'a penniless person' (بی‌چیز = without possessions, کس = person). را is the object/ topicalizing marker attached after کس. ندارند is the present/plural negative 'they do not have' used here with ارز (value) as the predicate: literally 'a penniless person has no value (to anyone).' The word order reflects poetic inversion: 'کس را ندارند ارز' = 'they have no worth for the person.'

sentences.s559-s29: Now I will tell the affair of Bahram the Bahramid; you listen, with heart and soul.
sentences.s559-s21: If you keep the world content by justice, you will remain wealthy and be joyful because of (your) justice.
sentences.s559-s22: Everyone needs security and uprightness; there should be no lack of justice within.
sentences.s559-s23: When joy is slightly lessened, the spirit is weakened, and wisdom becomes diminished among the powerless.
sentences.s559-s24: When his reign came to twenty years, (being a little short of life) he wept.
sentences.s559-s5: For a week he strove hard with him, and he continued so until he sat upon the throne.
sentences.s559-s6: When Bahram sat upon the throne, he administered justice; according to the custom of the Kayanids he placed the crown upon his head.
sentences.s559-s7: First he gave praise to the Creator, the illuminator of the turning of time,
sentences.s559-s8: increaser of knowledge and righteousness, remover of crookedness and deficiency.
sentences.s559-s1: When Bahram was in mourning for Bahramshah, he did not put a hat on his head for forty days.
sentences.s559-s2: Many troops departed, their minds full of pain, with moans and with cries.
sentences.s559-s3: They sat with him in mourning and in sorrow; their faces were pale and their lips had turned lapis-blue.
sentences.s559-s4: Thereupon a mobed of pure repute arose, to set the king upon the throne.
sentences.s559-s17: A man who has nothing does not attain muruwwat (chivalry/generosity); that very rank is not near his person either.
sentences.s559-s18: If you are content, you will become comfortable in body; but if you practise greed, you will become fearful.
sentences.s559-s19: Nor will one, by toil, attain that which is worth striving for; he turns the soul toward treasure out of greed.
sentences.s559-s20: In the affairs of the age choose the middle course; if you wish to obtain it by justice.
sentences.s559-s25: That merchant-king was joined with the earth; a tomb was hidden for him away from the joyful world.
sentences.s559-s26: Such are the world's customs and tunes; death does not loosen its grasp on anyone.
sentences.s559-s27: He had one son, a fortunate one, who bore the name Bahram Bahramian.
sentences.s559-s28: He came and sat upon the joyous throne, and placed the Kayanian crown upon his head.
sentences.s559-s9: The Lord of Kayvān and the revolving heavens asks nothing of a servant except justice and affection.
sentences.s559-s10: Thereupon he said thus: 'O wise ones, worldly‑wise and pure‑hearted mōbeds,'
sentences.s559-s11: Whichever of you has great learning, do not consort with mighty kings.
sentences.s559-s12: In the chronicles there appeared a man of wisdom; he was enlightened and a nurturer of the people.
sentences.s559-s13: His foremost quality toward the people was forbearance; when he grew hot-tempered, he subjected himself to humiliation.
sentences.s559-s14: Whoever became secure was glad; sorrow and suffering were dispelled along with that safety.
sentences.s559-s15: More wealthy is the one who possessed a generous heart; he was in the habit of scattering coins from the heart.
sentences.s559-s16: If you have nothing, give a little; for a penniless person is worth nothing to anyone.
